Title: Root microbiota drive direct integration of phosphate stress and immunity

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential macronutrient for plant growth and nutrient homeostasis
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: spx1 spx2 mutants show Pi hyperaccumulation-related PSR phenotypes and assemble a distinct root microbiome different from wild type and phr1
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SPX1/SPX2 were used as antagonistic PSR regulators in microbiome experiments. 2. spx1 spx2 plants carried distinct root bacterial communities in wild soil. 3. Their microbiome effect reflected PSR signaling type rather than simple shoot Pi accumulation level.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: These genes are part of the PSR regulatory network but were not the main mechanistic focus in the Results; they are included because mutant phenotypes were directly analyzed for microbiome effects.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Wild soil growth under phosphate-replete conditions
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant phenotyping; shoot Pi measurement; 16S root microbiome profiling
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Pi hyperaccumulation phenotypes and altered microbiome assembly may disturb homeostasis
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing, EBI SRA PRJEB15671

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential macronutrient for plant growth and nutrient homeostasis
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: spx1 spx2 mutants show altered PSR signaling and distinct root microbiome composition
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SPX2 was analyzed through the spx1 spx2 double mutant. 2. Loss of SPX1/SPX2 altered root bacterial community composition in wild soil. 3. The phenotype supports that PSR signaling regulators shape microbiome assembly independently of absolute Pi accumulation alone.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Analyzed only as part of spx1 spx2 double mutant.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Wild soil growth under phosphate-replete conditions
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant phenotyping; shoot Pi measurement; 16S root microbiome profiling
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altered Pi homeostasis and microbiome composition
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing, EBI SRA PRJEB15671

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Phosphate uptake and phosphate starvation response (PSR) pathway
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: upstream support step for Pi uptake
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: required for transporter delivery supporting Pi acquisition
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential macronutrient for plant growth and phosphate homeostasis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of PHF1 lowers shoot Pi and causes constitutive phosphate starvation response; mutant assembles an altered root microbiota and shows strong PSR marker induction under axenic low Pi
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. phf1 was used as a core PSR mutant with constitutively low Pi status. 2. In wild soil and SynCom systems, phf1 assembled a non-wild-type root microbiome. 3. phf1 showed strong induction of PSR marker genes, supporting that altered Pi-uptake machinery changes nutrition–microbiome interactions.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PHF1 is not the main mechanistic focus of the paper compared with PHR1/PHL1, but it is experimentally used as a core PSR gene affecting nutrient phenotype and microbiome assembly.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Low Pi versus high Pi; wild soil; synthetic bacterial community (SynCom) colonization
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant phenotyping; shoot Pi measurement; root microbiome profiling; RNA-seq of PSR marker genes
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Low Pi accumulation and constitutive PSR may alter microbiome composition and nutrient balance
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing, EBI SRA PRJEB15671; RNA-seq, GEO GSE87339

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Phosphate starvation response, Pi homeostasis, and regulation of immunity-associated metabolic outputs including glucosinolate biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Core PSR genes; IPS1; phosphate transport/homeostasis genes; a subset of SA-responsive defense genes; a subset of JA-responsive genes including glucosinolate biosynthetic genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Direct transcriptional activation of PSR genes and direct transcriptional repression or fine-tuning of immune genes via promoter binding
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates canonical PSR genes under low Pi and SynCom conditions; represses many SA-responsive defense genes; supports activation of a subset of JA-responsive/glucosinolate biosynthetic genes
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Master regulator of phosphate starvation response and decisive integrator connecting phosphate stress with immune metabolic output
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Low phosphate; presence of a live synthetic root bacterial community; phosphate stress context
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Likely functions with weakly redundant paralog PHL1; direct complex not established in this paper
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Shoot for Pi; roots/leaves not specifically assigned here for immune metabolites
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pi supports plant growth and nutrition; glucosinolates contribute to defense
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: PHR1 is required for microbiome-enhanced PSR activation under low Pi and directly suppresses immune outputs; loss of PHR1 enhances defense gene expression and disease resistance while impairing canonical PSR
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PHR1 is required for SynCom-enhanced activation of phosphate starvation marker genes under low Pi. 2. phr1 and phr1 phl1 mutants assemble altered root microbiomes in wild soil and SynCom systems. 3. ChIP-seq and motif analyses support direct promoter occupancy of many PSR and immunity-related genes by PHR1. 4. Loss of PHR1 elevates SA/flg22-responsive defense outputs and confers enhanced resistance to Hyaloperonospora arabidopsidis and Pseudomonas syringae. 5. PHR1 also promotes expression of a subset of JA-responsive genes, including glucosinolate biosynthetic genes, showing context-specific immune tuning.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper emphasizes direct integration of nutrition and immunity by PHR1. PHR1 is not presented as a general master regulator of all JA responses, but rather as a context-dependent fine-tuner during PSR and microbiota exposure.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Root-expressed; function assessed in seedlings under low Pi, SynCom exposure, BTH/SA, MeJA and flg22 treatments
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Low Pi versus high Pi; synthetic bacterial community; axenic low Pi with sucrose; MeJA; BTH/SA analogue; chronic flg22; pathogen infection
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; RNA-seq; ChIP-seq using PromPHR1:PHR1-MYC; promoter motif enrichment; shoot Pi measurements; root microbiome profiling; pathogen assays
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value target for engineering phosphate-use efficiency and microbiome interactions, but deployment requires caution because boosting PHR1 activity may suppress immunity; useful in context-specific tuning rather than constitutive overactivation
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Enhanced PHR1-dependent PSR may repress defense and increase disease susceptibility; loss of PHR1 impairs PSR and microbiome-associated Pi benefits
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq GEO GSE87339; hormone-response RNA-seq GEO GSE90077; 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing EBI SRA PRJEB15671; ChIP-seq reported, dataset to be presented in de Lorenzo et al.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Phosphate starvation response and associated immunity coordination
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Canonical PSR genes together with PHR1
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional regulation as a weakly redundant paralog of PHR1
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Supports activation of PSR marker genes; contributes with PHR1 to repression of defense outputs under phosphate stress
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Important co-regulator required with PHR1 for full PSR induction by the SynCom
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Functional redundancy with PHR1; complex not established
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential macronutrient for growth and phosphate homeostasis
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of PHL1 together with PHR1 severely reduces PSR and shifts transcription toward enhanced immunity
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PHL1 acts as a weakly redundant paralog of PHR1 in PSR. 2. phr1 phl1 double mutants show severely reduced SynCom-triggered PSR. 3. phr1 phl1 displays elevated defense gene expression and increased resistance to pathogens, indicating that PHL1 contributes to the PHR1-mediated nutrition–immunity balance.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PHL1 is mainly analyzed in combination with PHR1 rather than independently.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Root-expressed in seedlings under Pi and SynCom treatments
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Low Pi versus high Pi; SynCom colonization; flg22; MeJA; pathogen challenge
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Double mutant analysis; RNA-seq; microbiome profiling; shoot Pi measurements; pathogen assays
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Enhanced PHR1/PHL1 activity may prioritize phosphate stress response over defense
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq GEO GSE87339; 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing EBI SRA PRJEB15671
